content.body: Source URL doesn't necessarily need to be the source of identity, but Source Text only has its own problems, especially if a site isn't properly secured and can load arbitrary javascript. they could theoretically import a module with identical source text but its imports point to different locations.
If you have two files that both only contain `export const x = 1;`, would those compare identically?
content.formatted_body: If you have two files that both only contain export const x = 1;, would those compare identically?

content.body: right, so provided whitespace and comments match, under the current spec yes
so the equality survives serialization
it does also do an equivalence of the URL in addition
Do imports not matter in that case?
import maps would not be part of that identity model

content.formatted_body:
I'm actually very surprised that the example in the explainer doesn't maintain source url. If you have

// main.js
import source myModule from "./foo/bar/my-module.js";
const worker = new Worker(myModule);

// foo/bar/my-module.js
import { x } from "../shared.js";

// foo/shared.js
export const x = 1;

The module loaded by the worker would throw because the import can't be resolved?

content.body: No, the worker would separately import `/foo/shared.js`. Only `foo/bar/my-module.js` is serialized. Only with module declarations can the whole graph can be serialized. This avoids trying to serialize "instance state" / "linkage state".
content.formatted_body: No, the worker would separately import /foo/shared.js. Only foo/bar/my-module.js is serialized. Only with module declarations can the whole graph can be serialized. This avoids trying to serialize "instance state" / "linkage state".

content.body: Instead `new Worker(myModule)` sets up the worker with the same import map
content.formatted_body: Instead new Worker(myModule) sets up the worker with the same import map

(likely requires something like Node.js's data argument)

and imported, and imports are then loaded on the other side
in addition `new Worker(myModule)` when setting up the worker itself provides the import map from the parent into the worker's initialization

content.body: Yes, the module source is associated with its URL
Then I'm confused, as it seemed you were saying that the url isn't tied to the source. Or is it just that the url isn't used for comparison?
the URL is not a unique key - this was an early design decision to ensure that `import(source)` always gives the instance for that source, and not _another source_
content.formatted_body: the URL is not a unique key - this was an early design decision to ensure that import(source) always gives the instance for that source, and not another source

content.body: the source has its own unique key

If so, that seems potentially dangerous as comparing on source text alone but with a different source location could allow an attacker to forge a module that might compare identically with another module, but loads relative imports from its URL that contain their own code.
we compare on BOTH source and URL (host defined) currently
See https://tc39.es/proposal-esm-phase-imports/#sec-modulesourcesequal

But then what was the normative change that was proposed? If the identity of a module is based both on source text and source url, I think that would be acceptable for shared structs as well, preferred even.
because we cannot define a source identity based on zero motivation
and there was zero motivation from shared structs for seeing this
Is this not appropriate motivation?
the proposed identity change is module source _object identity_
content.formatted_body: the proposed identity change is module source object identity

content.body: which cannot be forged, as it is then an object capability model
The current spec status quo is URL + source text, which is unforgeable as source text URLs are internal and not possible to provide.
so I don't see the concern either way though

content.body: I think the identity arguments come from a place of cross-agent identity of sources not being seen as a crucial requirement - that is, even though possible to specify, it is work
for example - module declarations require de broglie indexing to uniquify them, evaluated modules require a new separate indexing to identify them as distinct (e.g. `eval('module foo {}; foo')`)
content.formatted_body: for example - module declarations require de broglie indexing to uniquify them, evaluated modules require a new separate indexing to identify them as distinct (e.g. eval('module foo {}; foo'))

content.body: this is all a lot of work that is hard to specify as it exists between specs
therefore if this work is to be fully undertaken it must be fully justified and motivated
not just assumed

the easiest round-trippable identity module remains an internal UUID as I mentioned, but the issue we have is the lack of existence in the spec today of any concept of a unique serializable generated ID being used for identity

The design for shared structs mandates some way to correlate the same declaration in two threads. If it's not something built-in to module source now, its something we will have to solve to make progress
so this was the premise of the normative change - that structs was not interested in this identity model
if we have a genuine argument being made that structs is indeed wanting this identity model, then that does change the assumptions

Yes. In some form, shared structs need a way to correlate a `shared struct` declaration in the same file loaded in two separate agents. Otherwise there exists the potential for a malicious third party to forge a declaration that exposes private state. The mechanism we've discussed for this has been to leverage the source url within the module resolution cache, though we would likely also need to verify that the sources are unchanged. This reduces the attack surface for a third party to maliciously intercept, and ensures there is no new registry with which one could introduce a side channel between two isolated scripts.
content.formatted_body: Yes. In some form, shared structs need a way to correlate a shared struct declaration in the same file loaded in two separate agents. Otherwise there exists the potential for a malicious third party to forge a declaration that exposes private state. The mechanism we've discussed for this has been to leverage the source url within the module resolution cache, though we would likely also need to verify that the sources are unchanged. This reduces the attack surface for a third party to maliciously intercept, and ensures there is no new registry with which one could introduce a side channel between two isolated scripts.

content.body: By having this guarantee, we can ensure that a method defined on a shared struct in one thread is an equivalent method in another thread, and allow methods in either thread to interact with the private state of a shared struct.

this allows us to have shared private state that can be guarded using thread coordination primitives at the public boundary, which was something that was necessary for Mark Miller's buy-in to the proposal.

* This allows us to have shared private state that can be guarded using thread coordination primitives at the public boundary, which was something that was necessary for Mark Miller's buy-in to the proposal.

m.new_content.body: This allows us to have shared private state that can be guarded using thread coordination primitives at the public boundary, which was something that was necessary for Mark Miller's buy-in to the proposal.

content.body: A shared struct instance has no behavior itself, as methods cannot be shared. It also doesn't precisely have a `[[Prototype]]` slot. Instead, a prototype walk of a shared struct looks up the associated prototype object within the current realm, similar to how we look up the prototype for a string or number. This allows each agent/realm to maintain an agent-local version of the prototype so that functions remain local and only the data is in shared memory.
content.formatted_body: A shared struct instance has no behavior itself, as methods cannot be shared. It also doesn't precisely have a [[Prototype]] slot. Instead, a prototype walk of a shared struct looks up the associated prototype object within the current realm, similar to how we look up the prototype for a string or number. This allows each agent/realm to maintain an agent-local version of the prototype so that functions remain local and only the data is in shared memory.

content.body: Here's a question - would you consider the same shared struct in different compartments to fulfill shared struct identity?
so the same underlying module sources defining the `struct` declaration
content.formatted_body: so the same underlying module sources defining the struct declaration

content.body: but in different evaluation compartments
We might need clearer terminology. Do you mean "same shared struct declaration" or "same shared struct instance"?
is declaration source equality not the same as shared struct instance equality? I guess that was my premise for source equality dependence?

If you mean reevaluating the module source in two different compartments, then you would have two independent evaluations of the declaration that are correlated between agents/compartments based on their source location. If you create a new instance of the shared struct in the first compartment it will have an associated identity tied to the declaration/source location from whence it was born. If you hand that struct to another compartment and evaluate within the context of that compartment, then any prototype walk would look for the declaration tied to the struct's type identity within that compartment.
At least, that's the case between agents and I believe we had discussions about the compartment model that aligned with this thinking.
I guess identity would need to be the combination of identities of the struct declaration and its parent declarations as well?
so that cross-module inheritance works out correctly
Parent declarations?
for `struct Foo extends Bar`
content.formatted_body: for struct Foo extends Bar

content.body: where `Bar` is imported from another module
content.formatted_body: where Bar is imported from another module

content.body: Ah, that doesn't actually matter.
If you have `shared struct Foo extends Bar` we only care about the declaration itself. the first step on the prototype walk will look up `Foo` and a regular prototype walk will find `Bar`.
content.formatted_body: If you have shared struct Foo extends Bar we only care about the declaration itself. the first step on the prototype walk will look up Foo and a regular prototype walk will find Bar.

content.body: Since we only correlate declarations based on location (and content, I suppose), we can be reasonably certain the walk is the same since we have other requirements around subclassing.
right so it can in theory work just fine from a shared memory perspective due to prototypal inheritance even if the contract of `Bar` is broken
content.formatted_body: right so it can in theory work just fine from a shared memory perspective due to prototypal inheritance even if the contract of Bar is broken

content.body: or rather `Bar`'s identity checks would fail explicitly?
content.formatted_body: or rather Bar's identity checks would fail explicitly?

content.body: have you considered plain structural identity on field count and field names? Has that been ruled out for optimization purposes?

How would the contract for `Bar` be broken? IIRC, in `shared struct Foo extends Bar`, `Bar` must also be a shared struct declaration. Shared structs can only be declared at the top level of a module, so that severely limits how they could be broken. Perhaps not entirely, but reliably enough.
content.formatted_body: How would the contract for Bar be broken? IIRC, in shared struct Foo extends Bar, Bar must also be a shared struct declaration. Shared structs can only be declared at the top level of a module, so that severely limits how they could be broken. Perhaps not entirely, but reliably enough.
